title: Natyashastra Rasa Theory and Classification
focus: Detailed exploration of Rasa theory, the classification of musical instruments, and the 'Fifth Veda' status of the Natyashastra.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 033]
The Natyashastra: Foundation of Indian Performing Arts
1. Ancient Origins
Attributed to sage Bharata, composed circa 2nd century BCE
2. Core Concepts
Drama (natya), expression (abhinaya), music (sangita), emotion (bhava)
3. Comprehensive Treatise
Contains approximately 36,000 verses detailing every aspect of performance
4. Rasa Theory
Introduced the revolutionary concept of aesthetic flavour and emotional essence
Living Traditions
- Bharatanatyam
- Kathak
- Kathakali
- Odissi
- Kuchipudi
Recognised globally as a seminal text on theatre, dance, and music — its influence extends far beyond India's borders.

[Page 034]
About Natyashastra (Science of Dramaturgy) - Also referred to as 'Fifth Veda', it was written by Bharatamuni in Sanskrit and was codified around the 2nd century BCE. - It comprises comprehensive set of rules that define nāṭya (drama), abhinaya (performance), rasa (aesthetic experience), bhāva (emotion), saṅgīta (music), etc. to create Rasa. - It is an epitome of Nāṭyaveda (also known as the Gāndharvaveda). - Natyotpatti, meaning "birth of performing art," refers to the origin story of the Natyashastra. - It is the first text available on the classification of India musical instruments.
evidenceBullets: The Natyashastra: Foundation of Indian Performing Arts
